coredata.dc:title: The flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO

Zhang, Xiufang

Xie, Shikui

Cui, Can
Li, Chaorong
coredata.dc:description: The flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots (C QDs)/ZnO were prepared by electrospinning-hydrothermal method. The results demonstrated that these films exhibited obvious enhancement of photosensitivity and flexible stability. Further, this enhanced photoconductivity and flexible stability could be attributed to C QDs with unique up-converted photoluminescence and cotton cellulose with flexibility and physical-chemical stability.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD spectra of the as-prepared samples, (b) the SEM of the sample, insert: high resolution SEM, (c) the TEM of the sample, (d) the HRTEM of C QD, (e) the HRTEM of ZnO.
(a) The optical transmittance spectra (T) of the samples with different ratio of C QDs, (b) the photosensitivity spectra, (c) the stability of flexible photosensitivity.
(a) Up-converted fluorescence spectra of C QDs, (b) schematic illustration for photoconductivity enhancement.

head.ce:title: The flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots (C QDs)/ZnO were prepared by electrospinning-hydrothermal method. The results demonstrated that these films exhibited obvious enhancement of photosensitivity and flexible stability. Further, this enhanced photoconductivity and flexible stability could be attributed to C QDs with unique up-converted photoluminescence and cotton cellulose with flexibility and physical-chemical stability.

ce:section.ce:para: The transparent conductive materials is expected to apply in scientific research field or daily life. Up to now, lots of encouraging results have been reported, such as transparency electrode, transparent cell phone or transparent solar cell [1,2]. Regrettably, most of the transparent conductive materials are nonflexible, which restricted the further development in transparent devices, for instance, flexible displayer or detector. So how to prepare flexible transparent conductive materials is a significant issue in electronic material field.

Now, lots of groups have explored the flexible conductive materials and plenty of achievements have been reported, such as Coleman [3] groups have prepared the highly flexible and transparent solid-state supercapacitors, Mathieu [4] et al. have prepared the conductive nanofibers by electrospinning, Kim [5] et al. have prepared silver nanodendrite/cellulose acetate thin film electrodes, and others [6]. Here the remarkable conductivity and stability of the substrate, the cost of the conductive materials would be the cores of the researches.

As known, the inexpensive and innoxious ZnO is considered as prominent transparent conductive materials with its wide bandgap and semi-conductive performance [7]. But due to its rigidity and frangibility, the ZnO is hardly used in such flexible design. Hence, how to deposit the ZnO on the flexible substrate would be a promise way. As recommended, the cotton cellulose framework (CCF) may be a commendable choice with its extensive sources and easy manufacture [8], what’s more, the high biocompatibility and environment friendly could make it be applied more widely. Reported by previously literatures, the CCF with larger specific surface area could provide adequate load sites for the growth of ZnO, and the toughness could make the CCF substrates for repeated bending [9]. Furthermore, the lightweight and thermostability are also advantages for its application in substrates.

However, the ZnO (bandgap of 3.2 eV) [10] with high transparent shows low photosensitivity in visible light. Though the narrow bandgap materials such as CdS or ZnS could provide high visible light response, they may also make the transmission light distortion. While, the carbon quantum dots (C QD) [11] with balanced absorption in visible light and unique up conversion fluorescence would be considered as potential material in transparent-conductive field. These properties are widely reported in photocatalysis, such as the visible-light photocatalysis of C QD modified TiO2 has been reported by Liu [12] groups, the enhanced photocatalysis of up-conversion C QD/TiO2 has been prepared by Li and Wang [13] groups, etc. [14,15], but it is rarely applied in flexible transparent materials.

In this work, we prepared the flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO via simple routes of electrospinning-hydrothermal method. The results show that the films with high transmittance possess excellent photosensitivity. Furthermore, the flex resistance and the mechanism of enhancement of visible light photosensitivity are explored.

The CCF was obtained by electrospinning process according to our previous work [8] and the ZnO nanorods were prepared by hydrothermal synthetic method (aqueous solution of 25 mM Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 25 mM HMTA, 90 °C, 10 h).
The carbon quantum dots were prepared by hydrothermal synthetic method (0.75 g sucrose, 30 mL DI water, 180 °C, 5 h).
The samples coupled by different ratio of C QDs are labelled as C-Zn-C-0, C-Zn-C-1, C-Zn-C-2, C-Zn-C-3 (80 °C, 8 h). (The detail and characterization are shown in ESI).

The XRD spectra of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO (CCF/C QDs/ZnO) with different ratio are shown in Fig.1 a. As revealed, all the samples exhibit similar peaks of (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201), which is corresponding to typical diffraction peaks of ZnO (PDF-36-1451). However, due to the small grain size and feeblish crystallinity of the C QD, the peaks of the C QDs could be hardly observed. Fig.2 b shows that the CCF/C QDs/ZnO is consisted of villiform nanofibers, which is uniform and bushy. The insert is the high resolution SEM, which shows that the villiform branches are formed by the ZnO nanorods with diameter of 60–80 nm. Restricted by the resolution of the SEM and the grain size of the C QDs, it’s hard to observe the C QDs on the surface of the ZnO nanorods, which would be proved in TEM. Fig.1c is the TEM of the as-prepared CCF/C QDs/ZnO. As shown in Fig.1a, the C QD has attached on the surface of the ZnO nanorod. Further, the HRTEM of the sample reveals that the lattice spacing of 0.320 nm (Fig.1d) is ascribed to the (002) plane of C QD [12] and the lattice spacing of 0.248 nm (Fig.1e) is ascribed to the (101) plane of ZnO [16], respectively.

As shown in the FT-IR spectra (ESI Fig. S1), the peak at 1381 cm−1 of the epoxy C–O stretching vibration merely appearing in CCF/C QDs/ZnO films, which could be explained by the successful interactions between ZnO nanorods and C QDs. Further, as revealed in the XPS (ESI Fig. S2), the main peak (Fig. S2c) at 284.6 eV corresponds to the C–C bond with sp2 orbital. The peaks locate at 286.2 and 288.0 eV pertain to the C–O and C–O bonds, respectively. The peaks locate (Fig. S2d) at 529.2, 529.9 and 531.4 eV are assigned to ZnO, CO and COH. Both Figs. S2c and S2d indicate that there have formed carbon-oxygen bonds between the C QDs and ZnO [14].
All above results convince that the C QDs have attached on the surface of the ZnO nanorods.

The optical transmittance spectra of the samples with different ratio of C QDs are displayed in Fig.2a. As revealed, the films exhibit highly visible transmittance of around ∼75% in average, and the transmittance decreased slightly with increasing C QDs, which could be ascribed to the visible light absorption of C QDs [16]. As shown, the insert is the actual photograph, which displays a high transmittance and undistorted tint under the visible light. What is more, there is an obviously decrease about 380 nm for all samples, which could be ascribed to the bandgap of ZnO [10]. It is noteworthy that the absorption curves demonstrate similarly, which may indicate that the structures of the film-detectors barely distort after C QDs being introduced. As desired, the high transmittance and undistorted tint could help these films to be applied to the transparent detector.

As shown in Fig.2b, it is inspiring that the samples exhibit high photosensitivity after C QDs being introduced, which increases dramatically with the increasing ratio of C QDs from 1 × 0.5 ml to 2 × 0.5 ml, then slightly decreased. By calculating, the photocurrents are 2.59 × 10−8, 4.23 × 10−8, 5.52 × 10−8 and 4.79 × 10−8 A, respectively. The optimized value obtains at the C-Zn-C-2 imply that the suitable amount of C QDs modification could improve the visible light photosensitivity effectively.

The photoconductive stability with multiple bending is another important factor (Fig.2c). The insert is the flexibility of the as-prepared film, and the flexible photosensitivity is measured with different bending times of 0–300. As revealed, the as-prepared sample exhibits a remarkable photosensitivity of about 300%, and then decreased with the bending time. However, it’s interesting that the photosensitivity displays a stability value about 260% after 100 times until 300 times, which indicates that this film exhibit excellent flexible photosensitivity.Therefore, it is significant to explore the mechanism of photosensitivity in this structure. For this typical n-type semiconductor, the photoconductor is mainly from photo-generated electrons [17]. So the formula of photoconductivity could be simplified as
Δ σ = Δ n
μ
n
q
, where
q
,
Δ n
and
μ
n
denote the electron charge, changing of carrier concentration and electron mobility, respectively. Based on the XRD and TEM, the structures of the samples remain the same, so the electron mobility would be constant. Hence, the changing of photo-generated carrier concentration is the main reason for the photosensitivity.

Fig.3 a shows the up-converted fluorescence of C QDs with different excitation from 550 to 800 nm, there the up-converted emission with an excitation dependent behavior is located nearby 360 nm. As demonstrates in Fig.3b, parts of the visible light is converted to shorter wavelength (
λ ⩽ 380
nm
) with the C QDs being introduced, which could excite ZnO to increase photo-generated electrons, and be considered as the most important reason for the photoconductivity. What is more, compared with the traditional thin films, the three-dimensional kladodromous structure with re-absorption effect is deemed as another important reason for increasing the visible light utilization. Nevertheless, it is interesting that an excess amount of C QDs could lead a slight decreasing in photoconductivity. As previously literatures shown, limited by the quantum yield and itself conductivity [12], the excess C QDs covered on the surface of film-detector would result in a competition for light absorption, which may lead a decreasing in photocurrent. Moreover, the unmodified ZnO/CCNF film-detector also shows weakly photoconductivity, which could be ascribed to the intrinsic crystal defect of ZnO [18]. From that above, with the more efficiently visible light utilization and increasing photongenerated carriers, the CCF/CQDs/ZnO exhibit excellent photosensitivity of 265%.

We have successfully prepared flexible-transparent photosensitive films of cotton cellulose framework of carbon quantum dots/ZnO through a simple process and proved its excellent optical performance, photosensitivity and flexible stability, which could be mainly attributed to the unique up-converted fluorescence behavior of C QDs and the physical-chemical stability of the CCF. In addition, the three-dimensional kladodromous structure is another important reason.

In light of undistorted transmittance, high photosensitivity and flexible stability, the CCF/C QDs/ZnO films are expected to be ideal flexible-transparent photosensitive materials in future transparent devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51672249, 51603187 and 91122022), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. LQ17F040004 and LY15E030011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.10.005.
